meta.name: संस्कृतम्
meta.about: Classical language of India. Root of many modern languages.

vocab.note: One of the most important concepts in Indian philosophy — duty, righteousness, cosmic law. Masculine noun, a-stem.
Neuter noun. Literally 'action' — the concept of karma comes directly from this word.
From the root 'yuj' (to join/yoke) — same root as English 'yoke'! Masculine, a-stem.
Often repeated three times at the end of prayers — 'Om Shanti Shanti Shanti'.
a- (not) + hiṃsā (violence). Central concept popularized globally by Gandhi.
Masculine, a-stem. Root of the Surya Namaskar (sun salutation) yoga sequence.
Masculine, a-stem. 'रात्रौ' = at night (locative case of रात्रि).
Neuter, a-stem. 'जीवनम्' = life — a common phrase meaning 'water is life'.
Masculine, i-stem (irregular declension pattern, different from a-stem nouns).
Feminine, ī-stem. One of the five classical elements (pañca mahābhūta).
Irregular r-stem noun (related to Latin 'pater', English 'father' — same Indo-European root!).
Irregular r-stem noun, same family as Latin 'mater', English 'mother'.
Masculine, a-stem. 'विद्यालय' = school (vidyā=knowledge + ālaya=abode).
Feminine form of पुत्र. 'पठति' = reads (verb, 3rd person singular).
u-stem noun. Literally 'heavy/weighty (with knowledge)' — root of English 'guru'.
Number one. Declines like an adjective agreeing with the noun's gender.
Related to Latin 'duo', English 'two' — same Indo-European root visible across languages.
Related to Latin 'tres', English 'three'. Sanskrit numerals show deep Indo-European cognates.
Related to Latin 'quattuor'. Forms compounds like चतुर्भुज (four-armed, an epithet of Vishnu).
Related to Greek 'pente', English 'five'. 'पञ्चतन्त्र' = the famous fable collection Panchatantra.
Neuter, a-stem. Alternative word 'अक्षि' (akṣi) is also common for eye.
Masculine, a-stem. 'हस्तेन' = with the hand (instrumental case).
Masculine, a-stem. Root of 'पादहस्तासन' (a yoga pose name) — pāda+hasta+āsana.
Neuter, a-stem. Related to Latin 'cor/cordis', English 'cardiac' — deep cognate.
Neuter, s-stem (consonant stem, irregular declension). Root of 'मानव' (manava = human, the mind-possessing one).
Feminine, ā-stem. From root 'vid' (to know) — same root as English 'wisdom', 'wit'.
Neuter, a-stem. Root of Hindi 'किताब'-adjacent borrowings in many modern Indian languages.
Feminine, ā-stem. Used in all modern Indian languages with the same meaning.
Neuter, a-stem, from root 'likh' (to write/scratch). Nominalized action noun.
Compound: vidyā (knowledge) + ālaya (abode/house). 'बालकाः' = children/boys.
Masculine, a-stem. Alternative word: हस्तिन् (hastin, 'having a hand/trunk').
Masculine, a-stem. Related to Latin 'equus', English 'equine' — strong cognate.
Irregular o-stem noun, can be masculine (bull/ox) or feminine (cow) — context determines.
Masculine, a-stem. 'वनराजः' = king of the forest (vana+rāja compound).
Masculine, a-stem. From root 'sarp' (to creep/crawl) — same root as Latin 'serpens'.
Neuter, a-stem. su- (good) + kha (axle-hole) — originally meant 'good axle-fit', i.e. smooth-running.
Opposite of सुख: dus- (bad) + kha. Central concept in Buddhist and Hindu philosophy.
Neuter, a-stem. Common in names: 'Balaram' = one with strength of Rama.
Neuter, a-stem. 'सत्यं वद' = 'speak the truth' — a famous Upanishadic injunction.
Neuter, a-stem. 'जयति' = conquers/wins (verb, 3rd person singular present).
Masculine, a-stem. Also a name of Shiva as the destroyer of time. 'नाशयति' = destroys.
Indeclinable adverb (avyaya) — doesn't change form regardless of case or number.
Irregular n-stem noun, one of the trickiest declension patterns. 'प्रजाः' = subjects/people.
Neuter, a-stem, from root 'yudh' (to fight). Title word in the Mahābhārata's battle narratives.
Neuter, a-stem, from root 'śās' (to teach/rule). Found in compound names like योगशास्त्र.

vocab.ex: जगत् माया अस्ति इति केचित् वदन्ति।
vocab.note: Feminine, ā-stem. Core concept in Advaita Vedanta — the world as cosmic illusion.
Irregular n-stem, neuter. Different from 'ब्रह्मा' (Brahmā, the masculine creator god) — easy to confuse!
Masculine, a-stem, from root 'muc' (to release). One of the four traditional goals of human life (puruṣārtha).
Masculine, a-stem, sam- (together) + sāra (flowing) — literally 'flowing together/wandering'.
Irregular n-stem, masculine. Distinct from अहं (ahaṃ, 'I' the pronoun) — आत्मन् is the deeper philosophical self.

freq.note: 3rd person singular of 'अस्' (to be) — the most fundamental verb, like German 'sein'.
From root 'भू' (to become/be). Alternative to अस्ति, often used for change of state.
First person singular pronoun. Declines fully across all 8 cases — माम्, मया, मम etc.
Second person singular. No formal/informal distinction in classical Sanskrit, unlike German du/Sie.
Third person masculine singular. Also functions as demonstrative 'that (one)'.
Feminine form of सः. Sanskrit pronouns agree in gender like adjectives.
Neuter form. Famous from the Upanishadic phrase 'तत् त्वम् असि' (That thou art).
Unlike English 'and', च typically follows EACH word it connects, not just appearing once between them.
Basic negation particle, placed before the verb. Simple and unchanging — no conjugation.
Like च, वा often follows each option rather than appearing once between them.
Marks the end of a quotation or thought — Sanskrit has no quotation marks, इति serves this function.
Conditional particle, often paired with 'तदा' (then) in the following clause.
Pairs with यदि (if) to form if-then constructions.
Relative locative pronoun. Famous phrase: 'where there is dharma, there is victory'.
Interrogative 'where' — different word from यत्र (relative 'where').
Interrogative adverb 'how'. Common in greeting-adjacent questions about wellbeing.
Most common interrogative pronoun. 'किं नाम तव?' = 'What is your name?'
Declines like a pronoun across genders/cases. 'भवन्तु' = may they become (optative mood).
u-stem adjective. Root of Hindi/many Indian language words for 'much/very'.
Opposite of बहु. Common in compounds like अल्पाहार (light meal).

chunks.note: Universal greeting, same in formal and informal contexts. Literally 'namaḥ' (bow) + 'te' (to you).
'भवतः' = your (honorific, genitive case). Polite, respectful register throughout classical usage.
'मम' = my (genitive of अहम्). Same structure works for any name.
'भवतु' = optative mood of 'to be' — expresses a wish/blessing rather than a statement.
'असि' = 2nd person singular of अस् (to be). Informal version using त्वम् implicitly.
Literally 'again we-meet' — first person plural present of 'मिल्' (to meet/unite).
From root 'इष्' (to wish). Followed by an infinitive-like construction for 'I want to do X'.
Question particle 'किम्' at the start turns a statement into a yes/no question.
India's national motto, from the Mundaka Upanishad. 'एव' = indeed/alone (emphasis particle).

verbs.note: Present stem 'गच्छ-' looks different from the root 'गम्' — common irregularity in Class 1 verbs.
Regular Class 1 verb — present stem adds '-a-' to the root, very predictable pattern.
Class 6 verbs also add '-a-' in present stem, looking similar to Class 1 in this tense.
Famous in the phrase 'सत्यं वद' (speak the truth). Regular Class 1 conjugation pattern.
Suppletive verb — present tense uses root 'पश्' but past participle uses entirely different root 'दृश्'!
Class 5 verb — present stem adds '-no-/-nu-' to the root, a distinct pattern from Class 1.
One of the most important and irregular verbs — root of 'कर्म' (karma, action). Class 8, highly irregular.
Class 3 verb — reduplicates the root (दा → ददा-). Related to Latin 'dare', English 'donate'.
Regular Class 1. Root of words like 'नायक' (leader) and many Indian personal names.
Root of 'भवति' from the freq list above! Also root of 'भूत' (past/ghost) and 'भविष्य' (future).

fillers.note: Emphasis particle, attaches to the word it emphasizes. 'सत्यम्+एव' = truth alone/indeed.
Means 'also/too' when following a word, or 'even' for emphasis depending on context.
Mild contrast particle, softer than a strong 'but'. Placed after the contrasted word.
Emphatic/causal particle — adds certainty or implies a reason, common in verse for meter too.
Often signals reported/hearsay information — 'it is said that...'. Common in narrative texts.

conv.title: प्रथम परिचयः (First Introduction)
conv.scenario: Two people greeting each other for the first time
conv.tip: नमस्ते works in every context — formal, informal, religious, secular. It's the single most useful word to know.

lines.speaker: सीता
lines.en: Greetings Rama. My name is Sita.
lines.note: Names can be inserted directly after नमस्ते as a vocative-style address.

lines.speaker: सीता
lines.de: अहं मिथिलायां वसामि। भवान् कुत्र वसति?
lines.en: I live in Mithila. Where do you live?
lines.note: 'मिथिलायाम्' = in Mithila (locative case ending -āyām for ā-stem feminine nouns).

lines.speaker: राम
lines.note: Same locative pattern as मिथिलायाम् — both are ā-stem place names.

conv.title: गुरुशिष्यसंवादः (Teacher-Student Dialogue)
conv.scenario: A student asking a teacher about Sanskrit
conv.tip: The guru-shishya (teacher-student) relationship is central to classical Indian education and is reflected in much of the literature.

lines.speaker: शिष्यः
lines.en: Teacher, how should Sanskrit be learned?
lines.note: 'गुरुदेव' = respectful address to a teacher ('teacher-god'). 'शिक्षणीयम्' = should be learned (gerundive).

lines.speaker: गुरुः
lines.de: प्रथमं शब्दान् पठ, ततः व्याकरणं जान।
lines.en: First read the words, then learn the grammar.

lines.speaker: शिष्यः
lines.en: How many words should be learned?

lines.speaker: गुरुः
lines.en: Ten words per day are enough.

lines.speaker: शिष्यः
lines.note: Same gratitude expression we saw in chunks — works in any register.

conv.title: धर्मचर्चा (A Discussion on Dharma)
conv.scenario: Two scholars briefly discussing the meaning of dharma
conv.tip: Philosophical dialogue (saṃvāda) is a classical literary form — many Upanishads are structured exactly this way.

lines.speaker: अर्जुनः
lines.note: 'कः' = what/who (interrogative pronoun, masculine). 'इति' marks the end of the implied question.

lines.speaker: कृष्णः
lines.en: Dharma is the fulfillment of one's own duty.

lines.speaker: अर्जुनः
lines.en: How do I know my own duty?
lines.note: 'जानामि' = I know (1st person singular of 'ज्ञा', to know).

lines.speaker: कृष्णः
lines.de: आत्मानं जानीहि, ततः कर्तव्यं स्पष्टं भवति।
lines.en: Know yourself, then your duty becomes clear.
